refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999. Requires instrumentation of link health signals and timestamped logging.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The NGHE solution shall monitor and report link availability metrics in real-time.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The NGHE solution shall implement link redundancy or failover mechanisms to achieve ≥0.999 availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication path with high-reliability network infrastructure (fiber, dedicated bandwidth)
DP-B: Dual-path redundant communication (primary + secondary network, automatic failover on primary loss)
DP-C: Multi-carrier aggregation (simultaneous use of cellular, satellite, and private network; load-balancing and seamless switchover)
DP-D: Hybrid mesh network with local relay nodes and cloud-based link orchestration
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual-path redundant communication with automatic failover. Primary path uses dedicated/managed network (e.g., private LTE or fiber); secondary path uses alternative carrier or technology (e.g., satellite or public cellular). Automatic failover triggered by primary link loss detection (heartbeat timeout or signal quality threshold). Both paths carry Tasking (commands) and Telemetry (vehicle state) in parallel or switchover mode.

potential_sources: Coupling between link availability (FR-NGHE-010) and vehicle mobility (vehicle must be within coverage of both primary and secondary networks)
Coupling between failover logic (DP-B) and Safety Services (must ensure safety state is maintained during switchover; no command loss during transition)
Coupling between redundancy cost/complexity (DP-B) and project budget/schedule constraints
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is decoupled from specific vehicle design or teleoperation UI; it operates at the network/communication layer. However, vehicle must support dual-modem hardware and failover firmware logic. Safety Services must be aware of link transitions to prevent unsafe state during switchover.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Secondary network (satellite or alternative carrier) is available at all operational sites; risk: coverage gaps may exist in remote areas.
Assumption: Failover detection and switchover can complete in <100 ms without loss of critical commands; risk: switchover latency may exceed safety requirements.
Assumption: Dual-path infrastructure cost is acceptable to customer; risk: cost may exceed budget, forcing reversion to single-path design.
Assumption: Regulatory approval for dual-modem operation and spectrum use is obtainable; risk: licensing delays or restrictions.
Risk: Synchronization of state across dual paths during switchover; if primary and secondary diverge, vehicle may execute stale commands.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to explicitly specify: (1) failover detection mechanism (heartbeat interval, signal quality threshold), (2) switchover latency budget (<100 ms), (3) state synchronization protocol (e.g., sequence numbers, acknowledgments), (4) cost/benefit trade-off analysis vs. DP-A (single high-reliability path). Consider hybrid: DP-B for high-risk sites, DP-A for sites with excellent single-path coverage.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication infrastructure, failover logic, and link health monitoring for bidirectional command-and-telemetry exchange. Includes network interface hardware (modems, antennas), failover controller firmware, link state machine, and telemetry aggregation. Does NOT include vehicle control logic (owned by Vehicle module) or safety policy enforcement (owned by Safety Services module).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands from tele-operator (or autonomous planner) to vehicle; routed through Teleoperation module via primary or secondary path. Format: command ID, target action, parameters, sequence number.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link quality, failover status) streamed from Vehicle module through Teleoperation module to tele-operator console. Includes link availability metrics (uptime, failover events, latency).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services module sends safety constraints/inhibits to Teleoperation (e.g., 'block commands if link quality < threshold'). Teleoperation reports link loss events to Safety Services for safety state assessment.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical network connections (primary and secondary paths) managed by Teleoperation. Includes modem control, path selection, and failover signaling.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Dual-path redundancy, failover detection, and link monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces to Vehicle (for modem hardware) and Safety Services (for safety constraints). High containment.

Vehicle module owns the vehicle platform and control logic, but link availability is a system-level concern, not vehicle-specific. If Teleoperation logic is embedded in Vehicle firmware, it becomes tightly coupled and difficult to upgrade independently. High diffusion risk.

Safety Services owns safety policy and state management, but link availability is a network/infrastructure concern, not a safety policy. Assigning DP-B to Safety Services would blur the boundary between safety logic and communication logic. High diffusion risk.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-B here would leave implementation ambiguous and prevent clear ownership. Not a suitable IM boundary for detailed design.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-B (dual-path redundant communication with failover). It has the highest containment score (0.95) and lowest diffusion score (0.05). The module's scope naturally encompasses network infrastructure, failover logic, and link health monitoring. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become too large if it also owns modem hardware drivers and low-level network stack. Mitigation: delegate driver/stack to Vehicle module; Teleoperation owns only failover logic and link state machine.

Risk: Failover logic must be synchronized with Safety Services safety state machine. If link fails, Safety Services must know immediately to inhibit unsafe commands. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must be low-latency and reliable (e.g., shared memory or high-priority message queue).

Risk: Dual-path infrastructure (modems, antennas, network subscriptions) may be managed by different teams (hardware, network operations). Teleoperation module must define clear interfaces to these external systems. Mitigation: define modem control API and network provisioning contract.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module scope to explicitly exclude low-level modem drivers and network stack (owned by Vehicle or platform layer). Define SafetyAlert interface as a high-priority, low-latency channel (e.g., interrupt-driven or shared memory). Create a sub-module within Teleoperation for failover state machine and link health monitoring to improve clarity and testability.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual-path redundant communication with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach: primary + secondary paths with automatic failover. Redundancy is a proven technique for achieving high availability. No logical gap between requirement and design parameter.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: Failover logic may leak into Vehicle module if vehicle firmware must implement switchover logic. Mitigation: centralize failover state machine in Teleoperation; Vehicle firmware only reports link quality metrics.

Diffusion risk: Safety Services must be tightly coupled to link state for safety-critical decisions. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert interface as a formal contract with clear semantics (e.g., 'link_quality_degraded' event triggers safety inhibit).

Diffusion risk: Network infrastructure (modems, carriers, coverage) is external to NGHE solution; Teleoperation module depends on external provisioning. Mitigation: document assumptions about network availability and coverage in design specification.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-B. The module boundary is clear: it owns communication infrastructure, failover logic, and link monitoring. External interfaces are minimal and well-defined. No significant architectural diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-010 is feasible: (1) Instrument Teleoperation module to log link state transitions (up/down) with timestamps. (2) Run evaluation period (e.g., 30 days) in operational environment. (3) Calculate availability = (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). (4) Compare against acceptance criterion (≥0.999). Requires test harness and telemetry infrastructure, but no fundamental barriers.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≥0.999 availability. Sub-allocations to Vehicle (modem hardware, driver support) and Safety Services (safety constraints on link quality) are secondary and well-defined. No ambiguity about ownership.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B to include explicit failover detection mechanism (heartbeat interval, signal quality threshold) and switchover latency budget (<100 ms). This will improve verification feasibility.
Define SafetyAlert interface contract in detail: event types, latency requirements, and state synchronization protocol. This will reduce coupling risk between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Document assumptions about network infrastructure (coverage, availability, latency) and identify external dependencies. This will clarify scope boundaries and risk allocation.

Create a detailed design specification for Teleoperation module, including failover state machine, link health monitoring algorithm, and interface definitions. This will support detailed design and implementation.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command uplink, telemetry downlink, link monitoring, redundancy/failover). DP-B (dual-path redundant communication) is a sound design approach, and Teleoperation is the correct IM boundary with high containment (0.95). However, the design is not sufficiently detailed for implementation. The next level must decompose the sub-FRs and refine the failover logic, link monitoring algorithm, and interface contracts. Proceeding to detailed design without further decomposition risks ambiguity and integration issues.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with <500 ms latency and <1% packet loss.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with <1 second latency and <5% packet loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The Teleoperation module shall detect primary link loss within 5 seconds and automatically switch to secondary path without loss of critical commands.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The Teleoperation module shall monitor and report link availability metrics (uptime, failover events, latency, packet loss) to Safety Services and tele-operator console in real-time.
FR-NGHE-010.5: The Teleoperation module shall synchronize command state across primary and secondary paths to prevent duplicate or stale command execution.

assumptions: Assumption: Secondary network (satellite or alternative carrier) is available at all operational sites with ≥99% availability.
Assumption: Failover detection and switchover can complete in <5 seconds without loss of critical commands (e.g., emergency stop).
Assumption: Vehicle firmware supports dual-modem hardware and can report link quality metrics to Teleoperation module.
Assumption: Safety Services module can receive and act on link quality alerts within <100 ms.
Assumption: Tele-operator console can display link status and failover events to operator.
Assumption: Network infrastructure (modems, antennas, carriers) is provisioned and maintained by external teams; NGHE solution assumes availability.
open_risks: Risk: Secondary network coverage may have gaps in remote operational areas; mitigation: conduct site survey and coverage analysis before deployment.

Risk: Failover switchover latency may exceed safety requirements (e.g., emergency stop must complete in <100 ms); mitigation: implement hardware-based failover (e.g., dual-modem with automatic switching) to reduce latency.
Risk: State synchronization across dual paths may fail, leading to duplicate or stale command execution; mitigation: implement sequence number and acknowledgment protocol.
Risk: Dual-path infrastructure cost may exceed budget; mitigation: conduct cost-benefit analysis and consider phased deployment (single path initially, dual path for high-risk sites).
Risk: Regulatory approval for dual-modem operation and spectrum use may be delayed; mitigation: engage regulatory team early and identify alternative technologies (e.g., private LTE vs. satellite).
evidence_needed: Network coverage maps for primary and secondary paths at all operational sites.
Failover detection and switchover latency measurements from prototype or simulation.
State synchronization protocol specification and validation (e.g., formal verification or simulation).
Cost-benefit analysis comparing DP-A (single high-reliability path) vs. DP-B (dual-path redundancy).
Regulatory approval status for dual-modem operation and spectrum use.
Safety analysis (FMEA or FTA) for link loss scenarios and failover logic.
Interface specification for SafetyAlert, Tasking, Telemetry, and Network Link.
Test plan for link availability verification (e.g., 30-day evaluation period in operational environment).
